posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked:
Champagne Should Not Have To Be Decanted!

  • 1. Agree
  • 2. Disagree
  • 3. Agree in principle, but can tolerate an occasional exception.

0 voters

Inspired by the current Collin thread.

options.html: 3. Agree in principle, but can tolerate an occasional exception.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I never decant Champagne, and I never saw Collin, Bouchard, Selosse or any other grower decant Champagne either. Perhaps they do it when I’m not looking.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked: I do think some very young Bouchard does benefit from a brief decant because it can be very nervy. I used to decant some Collin, I do not think the current releases need it.

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked:
Greg and I have disagreed about this in other threads. I find that recent releases of Collin are much more forthcoming after a 60-90 minute decant.

Last week I was fortunate to drink ‘16 Enfers and Pierrieres twice at long tasting menu dinners where it was my only beverage (except for a glass or two of red wines to accompany courses), so I paid particular attention to how each evolved over several hours.

The wines were all at their best at the end of the evening, up to four hours after decanting, and they all were more open, richer, and better to my palate after 60-90 minutes.

Les Enfers didn’t fully open up and I preferred both bottles of Les Perrieres.
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
I do think some very young Bouchard does benefit from a brief decant because it can be very nervy. I used to decant some Collin, I do not think the current releases need it.

I’d go way further and say I’ve come to think it’s a mistake not to decant many Champagnes that are tight (and not only super young or recent releases), UNLESS one has hours and sometimes 1-2 days to let them open up in the glass or bottle.

For fans of decanting or long aeration in the glass, how does it work and what are you left with? I’ve found that after an hour or so, I’m basically left with flat wine. At that point, what is the point of drinking champagne other than enjoying leesy still wine?

posts.name: Peter J@ckel
posts.cooked: But, but, but…… the bubbles!

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:

Last week I was fortunate to drink ‘16 Enfers and Pierrieres twice at long tasting menu dinners where it was my only beverage (except for a glass or two of red wines to accompany courses), so I paid particular attention to how each evolved over several hours.

The wines were all at their best at the end of the evening, up to four hours after decanting, and they all were more open, richer, and better to my palate after 60-90 minutes.

Les Enfers didn’t fully open up and I preferred both bottles of Les Perrieres.

Have you done this with the bottles being open vs being decanted?

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
I never decant Champagne, and I never saw Collin, Bouchard, Selosse or any other grower decant Champagne either. Perhaps they do it when I’m not looking.

Anselme did used to ‘suggest it’ on his price list though.

“Nos Champagnes s’exprimeront pleinement après un séjour minimal d’une année dans vos caves,
ou une ouverture de une à deux heures à l’avance ou après carafage. Tp° 10°-12°.“

I never bother.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
For fans of decanting or long aeration in the glass, how does it work and what are you left with? I’ve found that after an hour or so, I’m basically left with flat wine. At that point, what is the point of drinking champagne other than enjoying leesy still wine?

that’s pretty surprising. It shouldn’t be terribly different from a wine that’s been poured multiple times through the night.

posts.name: Tim Williams
posts.cooked: I never decant champagne, I appreciate the bubbles. Even the last glass out of a bottle loses something for me.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
For fans of decanting or long aeration in the glass, how does it work and what are you left with? I’ve found that after an hour or so, I’m basically left with flat wine. At that point, what is the point of drinking champagne other than enjoying leesy still wine?

Could you clarify your question? For some champagnes (including those like Collin, Bouchard, Larmandier-Bernier, other growers) there aren’t a ton of bubbles to begin with, so while decanting will allow more of the bubbles to dissipate, that’s not a big difference.

I do agree that if what you’re really looking for are the bubbles, decanting makes no sense. But the more you talk to growers the more they openly say that they’re trying to make “great wine rather than great champagne”, so I’m not surprised to see a bit fewer bubbles - it’s less important to them.

posts.name: Marc Hauser
posts.cooked: Sorry to be pedantic, but, to confirm, “should not have to be” or “does not have to be” or “should never be”?

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked: Wait…Doesn’t Bouchard put his in a blender since he doesn’t like bubbles?

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked: The question is one I’ve had for many years and still I`ve not pulled the trigger and decanted any. One of my wine groups is about to embark on some decanting experiments and perhaps we add champagne to the project. It does make sense to me to do so for those big ones that are super tight early on; I’m thinking many of the 2008 vintage early releases, but I have not decanted any and still am questioning the potential benefits.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
The question is one I’ve had for many years and still I`ve not pulled the trigger and decanted any. One of my wine groups is about to embark on some decanting experiments and perhaps we add champagne to the project. It does make sense to me to do so for those big ones that are super tight early on; I’m thinking many of the 2008 vintage early releases, but I have not decanted any and still am questioning the potential benefits.

I wouldn’t have thought it would make a difference to many 2008s where, to me, the issue is a lack of integration rather than “tightness” - the wines simply haven’t come together yet. I think 2008 Comtes and Cristal will eventually be great, but giving them air won’t make the various components whole. YMMV of course!

Personally the champagnes I have decanted in recent years have been either young Bouchards or super reductive bottles, like the occasional 04 Comtes. I think decanting works for some things but not others. It’s a bit like people (not directed at you Blake!) posting about oak “blowing off” after a wine is open long enough, which confuses me to no end. But to each their own.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
I wouldn’t have thought it would make a difference to many 2008s where, to me, the issue is a lack of integration rather than “tightness” - the wines simply haven’t come together yet. I think 2008 Comtes and Cristal will eventually be great, but giving them air won’t make the various components whole. YMMV of course!

I think this is a really good and interesting point deserving of its own thread - the difference between tight and not-yet-integrated. I’ve been thinking about this for a long time. It’s one of the reasons I shake my head at the automatic assumption that long decants will help all young wines.

Anyway…I don’t decant champagne, but I do often observe it improving with air over the course of drinking a bottle.

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked:
Greg and I have disagreed about this in other threads. I find that recent releases of Collin are much more forthcoming after a 60-90 minute decant.

Last week I was fortunate to drink ‘16 Enfers and Pierrieres twice at long tasting menu dinners where it was my only beverage (except for a glass or two of red wines to accompany courses), so I paid particular attention to how each evolved over several hours.

The wines were all at their best at the end of the evening, up to four hours after decanting, and they all were more open, richer, and better to my palate after 60-90 minutes.

Les Enfers didn’t fully open up and I preferred both bottles of Les Perrieres.

Have you done this with the bottles being open vs being decanted?

Yes, but not side by side. Yet. Also, my standard approach to young, vinous Champagnes at home (where I’m the only drinker) is to pour out a glass, recork, and drink the bottle the next day or two. I find that they are better the next day(s) after opening.

I wonder about pouring half a bottle into a decanter and comparing the two halves over an evening. Perhaps I will do so as an ‘experiment’.

Edit: I agree with Sarah’s point and Greg’s that aeration is not a substitute in any manner for aging Champagne that is youthfully unintegrated. I also agree with Greg that Collin’s wines are ready to drink on release, but they are much better to my taste after decanting when they are young.
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.cooked:
For fans of decanting or long aeration in the glass, how does it work and what are you left with? I’ve found that after an hour or so, I’m basically left with flat wine. At that point, what is the point of drinking champagne other than enjoying leesy still wine?

Could you clarify your question? For some champagnes (including those like Collin, Bouchard, Larmandier-Bernier, other growers) there aren’t a ton of bubbles to begin with, so while decanting will allow more of the bubbles to dissipate, that’s not a big difference.

I do agree that if what you’re really looking for are the bubbles, decanting makes no sense. But the more you talk to growers the more they openly say that they’re trying to make “great wine rather than great champagne”, so I’m not surprised to see a bit fewer bubbles - it’s less important to them.

Actually, I think you got my point. As implied, I am new to Champagne (a year or two), so bubbles seem to be a significant part of the experience. And in reading the Champagne thread, the quality of the mousse is frequently referenced. That said, I have often found that the last glass of the bottle tastes the best, but that it is almost a still wine, so I see that as an “unfortunate” trade off. I have heard the grower comment you reference before and also that some are producing at lower atmospheres (sic?). That would suggest to me that there would be a significant move towards making Coteaux Champenois. Or does the very process of making Champagne (as opposed to the grapes and terroir) create a different wine even when it has gone flat (i.e. leesy still wine)?

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
I haven’t noticed any really issue with bubbles dissipating with a careful slow decant. (I don’t recommend shaking). I find the lost bubble concern to be overblown for my palate but recognize others may have a different experience. It’s worked well for Bouchard sure but also young Vilmart, young Roederer, young and old Peters, ….

On the correct observation that decanting won’t prematurely age a Champagne or integrate an entirely disjointed wine (one that is not merely tight): while true, once the top is popped, the distinctions don’t matter practically except in how to treat, or leave be, the next bottle. Decanting will either help or won’t. In any case I see only upside personally for bottles that might be tight or might be just disjointed/not ready. Again we are talking bottles that appear to be tight on opening even if they are really just not ready, not every bottle of young Champagne.

I also don’t usually do it if I’m at home. If there’s a Champagne that needs air there after trying a glass, I’ll just put a stopper on the bottle after pouring and stick it in the fridge for a day or two.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
For fans of decanting or long aeration in the glass, how does it work and what are you left with? I’ve found that after an hour or so, I’m basically left with flat wine. At that point, what is the point of drinking champagne other than enjoying leesy still wine?

Could you clarify your question? For some champagnes (including those like Collin, Bouchard, Larmandier-Bernier, other growers) there aren’t a ton of bubbles to begin with, so while decanting will allow more of the bubbles to dissipate, that’s not a big difference.

I do agree that if what you’re really looking for are the bubbles, decanting makes no sense. But the more you talk to growers the more they openly say that they’re trying to make “great wine rather than great champagne”, so I’m not surprised to see a bit fewer bubbles - it’s less important to them.

Actually, I think you got my point. As implied, I am new to Champagne (a year or two), so bubbles seem to be a significant part of the experience. And in reading the Champagne thread, the quality of the mousse is frequently referenced. That said, I have often found that the last glass of the bottle tastes the best, but that it is almost a still wine, so I see that as an “unfortunate” trade off. I have heard the grower comment you reference before and also that some are producing at lower atmospheres (sic?). That would suggest to me that there would be a significant move towards making Coteaux Champenois. Or does the very process of making Champagne (as opposed to the grapes and terroir) create a different wine even when it has gone flat (i.e. leesy still wine)?

I’m no expert, but I find that the bubbles are a bigger part of the experience in lesser Champagnes, as they would be in lesser sparkling wines, and can mask a lower quality of wine. Better Champagnes stand on their own well enough that an overly assertive mousse can be a distraction more than an enhancement. Also, as Charlie alludes, these wines aren’t really flat after air, or even a night open in the refrigerator. They still have bubbles, and the energy driving those bubbles, just fewer. I think the difference between Champagne and still wine is much greater than you are suggesting. That is to say, I don’t think a lower atmosphere Champagne or one that has been open / decanted approaches the same wine had it not been made as Champagne.

This is a wine geek/elite site. But if you said “Champagne,” I guess the first thought of most people would be bubbles, including most wine drinkers. To date, I drink only Grower Champagne, certainly not top-end stuff, but all producers highly respected on this board, so masking lower quality is not an issue. But I get your point, just as oak is used as a disguise. And I am not really suggesting anything about the difference - I am more asking questions, such as the one at the end of my last post.

posts.cooked: I have decanted Champagne before, but I have also decanted white Burgundy too so maybe I am just crazy. I don’t do it often, but it has worked well for me with young BdN.

posts.name: Michael  Passo
posts.cooked: I think champagne of age or an ager drunk early needs some air. Dare I say a champagne “Audouze”. Don’t worry about the bubbles, unlike colas, the bubbles in champagne last a lot longer than most people think, and yes it is wine that as been sleeping. Let it wake up!

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:

I decant a fair amount of white burgundy, but it’s not really about reduction to me (I don’t love reduction in whites), but about letting the aromas and flavors open.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked:
I have decanted Champagne before, but I have also decanted white Burgundy too so maybe I am just crazy. I don’t do it often, but it has worked well for me with young BdN.

I usually decant white Burgs; in fact, a good friend whose wine knowledge and expertise I deeply respect, had me decant 2 of his 2000 Grand Cru while he prepped another bottle with the instructions to gently, but repeatedly swirl both while in the decanters over 20 minutes or so. Never done that and wondered what the outcome would be. Not sure about what did what, but they were super ready and stupendous.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked:
I’ve found the wine community at large fusses over the bubbles in Champagne far more than it ever needs to. Young méthode champenoise wines hold their bubbles much longer than most assume they will. I think people have memories of experiences with cheap bubbles and sodas going flat quickly and assume Champagne will work the same. That’s an obvious assumption to make but it doesn’t really hold.

I used to fuss over closing my Champagne over night…well, because everyone else did. But after forgetting to stopper bottles more than once and noticing no real change in the effervescence over 24 hours on nearly every occasion, I’ve stopped trying to close them in the fridge at all. I’m drinking the bottom half of a NV Paul Bara rosé right now that is drinking just like the top half did last night when I put it in the fridge un-stoppered.

Of course this shifts with age as those bubbles do eventually fade with time. I wouldn’t treat a 20+ year old Champagne the same way. Hopefully I’ll never bother keeping one overnight either.

For younger wines I can’t imagine gentle decanting being much different. Pouring the wine is not the same as ridding it of its effervescence. If that were true, then many of the glasses we drink would be showing some loss of mousse.

posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
I never decant Champagnes, so I’m curious about it.

What sorts of Champagnes do you all think would benefit most from decanting? Young, I’m assuming. But, what else? If you were buying a bottle that you’d never had before, what sort of characteristics would lead you think it might benefit from a decant? NV vs vintage? BdB? BdN? More or less expensive? Grower vs House? Dosage level? Something else?

Thanks!

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Anyway…I don’t decant champagne, but I do often observe it improving with air over the course of drinking a bottle.

This is where I come out on it too. Pouring into a wide decanter seems pretty out there. I guess if it were a tight young champagne and for some reason you had to pour it for a table full of people right away maybe?
They make Champagne decanters, Chris.

I’ve found the wine community at large fusses over the bubbles in Champagne far more than it ever needs to. Young méthode champenoise wines hold their bubbles much longer than most assume they will. I think people have memories of experiences with cheap bubbles and sodas going flat quickly and assume Champagne will work the same. That’s an obvious assumption to make but it doesn’t really hold.

I used to fuss over closing my Champagne over night…well, because everyone else did. But after forgetting to stopper bottles more than once and noticing no real change in the effervescence over 24 hours on nearly every occasion, I’ve stopped trying to close them in the fridge at all. I’m drinking the bottom half of a NV Paul Bara rosé right now that is drinking just like the top half did last night when I put it in the fridge un-stoppered.

Of course this shifts with age as those bubbles do eventually fade with time. I wouldn’t treat a 20+ year old Champagne the same way. Hopefully I’ll never bother keeping one overnight either.

For younger wines I can’t imagine gentle decanting being much different. Pouring the wine is not the same as ridding it of its effervescence. If that were true, then many of the glasses we drink would be showing some loss of mousse.

The two of us split a bottle of Laherte Ultradition tonight over the course of three hours, having three glasses each in Grassi Champagne glasses. It was delicious with a lively mousse at the beginning of each pour and each glass lasted about 45 minutes. No swirling. The bottom of each glass was essentially a still wine - still delicious but almost devoid of mousse altogether. I’ve had this experience numerous times. That is not making a fuss or an assumption. It is just my experience.

It is just my experience.

Maybe very small quantities, warming to room temperature, over longish periods of time do lose their bubbles. I can only relate what I told you above. I cannot vouch for any particular, idiosyncratic treatment people want to try with their wines. Just under normal treatment, opening, pouring, leaving chilled, the wines do not lose bubbles for me.

Of course this begs the question, why are you pouring Champagne into glasses and not drinking it for 45 minutes?

I said the glasses lasted 45 minutes, not sat untouched for 45 minutes. You are the one who suggested that there wouldn’t be a loss of mousse in the glass. If drinking a glass of Champagne over 45 minutes is idiosyncratic treatment to you, then let’s leave it at that.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
I’ve found the wine community at large fusses over the bubbles in Champagne far more than it ever needs to. Young méthode champenoise wines hold their bubbles much longer than most assume they will. I think people have memories of experiences with cheap bubbles and sodas going flat quickly and assume Champagne will work the same. That’s an obvious assumption to make but it doesn’t really hold.

I used to fuss over closing my Champagne over night…well, because everyone else did. But after forgetting to stopper bottles more than once and noticing no real change in the effervescence over 24 hours on nearly every occasion, I’ve stopped trying to close them in the fridge at all. I’m drinking the bottom half of a NV Paul Bara rosé right now that is drinking just like the top half did last night when I put it in the fridge un-stoppered.

Of course this shifts with age as those bubbles do eventually fade with time. I wouldn’t treat a 20+ year old Champagne the same way. Hopefully I’ll never bother keeping one overnight either.

For younger wines I can’t imagine gentle decanting being much different. Pouring the wine is not the same as ridding it of its effervescence. If that were true, then many of the glasses we drink would be showing some loss of mousse.

The two of us split a bottle of Laherte Ultradition tonight over the course of three hours, having three glasses each in Grassi Champagne glasses. It was delicious with a lively mousse at the beginning of each pour and each glass lasted about 45 minutes. No swirling. The bottom of each glass was essentially a still wine - still delicious but almost devoid of mousse altogether. I’ve had this experience numerous times. That is not making a fuss or an assumption. It is just my experience.

But that has to do with the amount of surface area being exposed in the small glass.

It’s not too dissimilar on how red wine oxidizes fairly quickly in larger wine bowls and your next pour is always fresher

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
I said the glasses lasted 45 minutes, not sat untouched for 45 minutes. You are the one who suggested that there wouldn’t be a loss of mousse in the glass. If drinking a glass of Champagne over 45 minutes is idiosyncratic treatment to you, then let’s leave it at that.

What Charlie said. Perhaps your solution is pour a bit less and top up more frequently. That’s typically my preference for most wines, if the situation allows.

posts.cooked:
Of course this begs the question, why are you pouring Champagne into glasses and not drinking it for 45 minutes?

To me it begs the question of why are you only opening a half bottle of Champagne?

Had a 2012 Cristal this past weekend that wouldn’t budge after a few hours, but was great the next day. So yes, I wish that could’ve been decanted.

Considering how many wine guys yank and explode their Champagne bottles open, I’m not surprised at the concern over loss of bubbles. Can’t blame the decanter for that though

posts.name: Joel D@vid$on
posts.cooked: Very interesting thread. I havent played much with decanting champagne, but i did decant about 1 glass worth of a 2015 Ulysse Collin Enfers 60mo recently. The wine was amazing and hard to argue against the decant making it the best. Although it lost most of it’s bubbles when I did, and tasted like one of the best white Burgundies I’ve had when decanted. But I’m still partial to the bubbles/mousse. Definitely worth more experimenting in the future.

posts.name: Jon H a r m s
posts.cooked: Decanting Champagne is just not something I want to do. I enjoy bubbles and they are an important part of the experience. If the mousse is too much, I’ll just swirl. I would also argue that you can pour a glass or two and allow it to open in the glass. What’s left in the bottle will have about the same surface area exposed to oxygen as in a carafe style decanter.

Depending on your preferences, quite possibly. The wine director said he wouldn’t hesitate to decant on this bottle. But since I had plenty of time and was drinking it mostly by myself, I chose to just let it get a decent amount of bottle air and try out of different glasses as well. If I did it again, I would use a big red burg glass, which they did not have at that restaurant. Or decant half the bottle and track the other half.

I don’t see all, or even many champanges holding up to this as well. But maybe I’ll prove myself wrong

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked: I think there’s some conflation here between letting a wine develop in the glass and decanting it. To me these aren’t the same thing, but ymmv. I don’t think the Enfers will lose much from a decant (I’ve not decanted my 60 month bottles) and there aren’t that many bubbles to lose anyway. The Enfers and the Roises are very dense wines (in the best sense of that word), so I’m not sure what the palate effect would do really be. Personally, the longer I’ve left Olivier’s wines in my glass the more the aromas come out (ginger in particular), but I’ve not noticed a particularly big shift on the palate. Am curious what people think after the decant though!

posts.name: Tran Bronstein
posts.cooked:
A truly fascinating thread. As an unabashed Champagne and Cremant lover, I have a few thoughts I’d like to share:

  1. The bubbles/mousse are an essential component of Champagne that sets it apart from all other wines. Champagne has the deserved reputation as the “fun wine.” The one you open up for celebrations. Any actions that could potentially lessen that component or, worse, turn the wine flat should IMHO be avoided at all costs.

  2. I’ve met many sommeliers here in Canada who have told me that Champagne should be treated with the same respect and care as a still wine. I see it from their POV, they have dealt with far too many people who open up fine Champagne only to down it like it were soda pop. A somm convinced fellow board and TWEC ™ member Jay Shampur to drink Champagne out of a Burgundy glass. He now never drinks it any other way and I see why. I’ve tried it and I agree it brings out a LOT more flavor and aroma in the wine – but at the severe cost of the wine’s bubbly mousse and texture. That kills a great deal of the joy of drinking Champagne for me.

Drinking Champagne out of my diamond shaped Schott-Zweisel flute glasses is more fun and the texture is preserved, but I wholeheartedly agree it constrains the nose and flavors far too much. So much so that I feel it’s actually a wonder Champagne tastes and smells as good as it does out of a flute glass given how restricted it actually is. It’s actually quite fascinating to drink Champagne out of a flute and out of a Burgundy glass side by side and seeing the difference. Given the choice between the two, I’d go with the flute style glass myself. This said, I’ve recently found that a great compromise is to drink out of a Cognac glass shaped beer snifter that gives me the best of both worlds. You may recall a thread I posted where I acquired barware from a neighborhood swap and acquired those beer snifters which are identically shaped but much smaller than a standard Cognac balloon glass.

  1. While I’ve never done it, I would have absolutely no fears about decanting a Champagne myself based on my experiences with it. On the occasions where I’m drinking a traditionally made Champagne or sparkling wine by myself, the bubbles last for days on end and the wine never goes flat. Doesn’t matter what kind of stopper I use to perserve it and even on occasion when I’ve actually forgotten a stopper completely. I’ve always found very minimal loss of bubbles and mousse myself to the point where I find it very hard to believe a normal decant would cause all the bubbles to dissipate. This said, if I were to decant a Champagne, I’d want to drink it all the very same night.

Had a recently purchased bottle of the Laherte Ultradition Brut on Friday. It surprised me by being much better after being open for 2-3 hours. posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
And the mousse is almost always intact

This. I’ve been served a Champagne blind. I managed to correctly identify the producer and guessed that the wine was opened the day before, seeing how the mousse seemed to be slightly less effervescent than usual.

I was told that yes, the wine was opened earlier, but it was much earlier than just one day - the wine had been sitting half-drunk, undisturbed, not stoppered (!) in the cold for six whole days. And the mousse was still there, and the wine was drinking like it was opened only a day before. An eye-opening experience on how well Champagne can survive if the opened bottle just remains in the cold and undisturbed the whole time.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
So @CFu and I put this theory to the test last night, decanting 2015 Ulysse Collin Les Enfers 60mo and 2015 Ulysse Collin Les Roises 60mo. I have been wanting to try Les Enfers 48mo or 60mo since reading the many notes about it here, and @William_Kelley 's praise as well, his more so on the Les Roises, and he actually pushed us to get that bottle along with the Les Enfers.

Both were decanted, and to showcase one of the new features of the forum (blur spoiler), I give you the results: (click the blurred text for the reveal)

Decanting Champagne CAN make a huge difference!

On the Les Enfers, little change was noticeable, but on Les Roises, that wine was basically 16 wines through the course of the evening with time in decanter. It started out reductive but also with a big ‘high toast’ component to me, but others didn’t seem to care. Throughout the evening it just kept changing, presenting you with different qualities along the way, keeping you interested and fascinated throughout. Without the decanter, I’m guessing the wine would still be wonderful, but it was a true treat to experience its evolution over the course of time, with all that air. Legendary.

IMG_45361920×2560 387 KB
